notes: Emergency medicine physician persona based in Houston metro area
Senior attending with trauma center experience

location_context.housing_situation: Owns a three-bedroom home in a suburban neighborhood within commuting distance of the medical center
location_context.community_ties: Member of a local Tamil cultural association; participates in hospital community outreach programs; neighbors are mostly young professional families

education_and_training.continuing_education_style: Attends ACEP annual conference; subscribes to Annals of Emergency Medicine; participates in simulation-based training quarterly

occupation_profile.work_setting: Level I trauma center in a large urban hospital; rotates between day and overnight shifts
typical_responsibilities: Triaging and stabilizing critically ill or injured patients
Performing emergency procedures (intubation, chest tubes, central lines)
Coordinating with surgical, cardiology, and ICU teams
Communicating with patients' families during crises
Documenting encounters in the electronic health record
occupation_profile.jurisdiction_or_regulatory_context: Texas Medical Board oversight; EMTALA obligations; Joint Commission accreditation standards; CMS documentation requirements
work_constraints: Must make rapid decisions with incomplete information
Emotional burden of pediatric trauma cases
Balancing teaching residents with patient throughput
Frequent exposure to infectious diseases and bodily fluids

socioeconomic.insurance_or_benefits_context: Employer-sponsored health, dental, and vision insurance; 403(b) retirement plan with hospital match; short-term disability coverage

communication_style.tone: Calm, efficient, and reassuring under pressure; can become clipped when fatigued
trigger_phrases: can't you just give me antibiotics
taboo_or_sensitive_topics: A pediatric patient she lost during her second year of practice
Guilt about time away from her daughter due to shift work

core_values: Equitable care regardless of insurance status
motivators: The adrenaline and challenge of high-acuity cases
Mentoring residents who remind her of her own early training
Making a tangible difference in a single shift
Earning the trust of patients in their most vulnerable moments
fears_and_anxieties: Making a diagnostic error that costs a life
Burnout eroding her empathy over time
Missing critical developmental moments with her daughter
Being named in a malpractice suit
moral_red_lines: Will never discharge a patient she believes is clinically unsafe to leave
Refuses to order unnecessary tests solely for defensive medicine
Will not tolerate disrespectful treatment of nursing staff by anyone
sources_of_pride: Completing a competitive emergency medicine residency
Being selected as attending of the year by residents
Successfully resuscitating a cardiac arrest patient who walked out of the hospital

health_and_wellbeing.general_health_context: Physically fit from intentional exercise routine; manages circadian rhythm disruption from shift work with discipline
stressors: Rotating day/night shifts disrupting sleep and family time
Emotional weight of patient deaths especially in pediatric cases
Administrative documentation burden cutting into clinical time
Navigating hospital politics around staffing levels
coping_strategies: Cooking elaborate South Indian meals as a creative outlet
Bi-weekly video calls with her parents and sister
Debriefing with a trusted colleague after difficult cases
health_and_wellbeing.sleep_and_energy_pattern: Highly variable due to shift rotation; uses blackout curtains and melatonin after night shifts; averages 6–7 hours but quality varies significantly

habits: Arrives 15 minutes before shift to review the board
Carries a personal stethoscope and penlight at all times
Writes brief patient narratives for teaching cases in a private journal
Checks labs and imaging results compulsively even after handoff
hobbies_and_interests: South Indian cooking (especially dosas and biryanis)
Bharatanatyam dance (practices occasionally, performed in college)
media_diet: EM:RAP and Taming the SRU podcasts
Tamil and Hindi films on streaming services
behavioral_profile.social_circle: Close-knit group of fellow EM physicians; strong relationship with her husband (a software engineer); stays connected with college friends through a group chat; warm but formal with hospital administration
biases_to_watch: Availability heuristic after seeing a rare diagnosis recently
Premature closure on a diagnosis when the ED is busy
Authority bias — may defer too readily to senior surgeons on ambiguous consults

backstory.one_paragraph_summary: Priya Ramanathan was born and raised in suburban Houston to immigrant parents who ran a small accounting practice. She decided on medicine after volunteering at a free clinic during high school and never wavered. After completing medical school in Texas and residency in a high-volume urban trauma center, she returned to Houston as an attending. Becoming a mother four years ago reshaped her priorities, and she now wrestles with the tension between the career she loves and the family time she craves. She channels this tension into mentoring residents and advocating for better physician wellness programs at her hospital.
formative_events: Volunteering at a free clinic in high school and seeing uninsured patients turned away
A harrowing pediatric resuscitation during residency that ended in the child's death
The birth of her daughter, which fundamentally shifted her relationship with risk
Being selected to lead a hospital-wide sepsis protocol redesign
current_challenges: Negotiating a shift schedule that allows more predictable family time
Managing compassion fatigue after a particularly heavy trauma season
Balancing clinical work with a growing interest in medical education leadership
Student loan repayment while saving for her daughter's education
near_term_goals: Transition to a schedule with fewer overnight shifts
Publish a case series on point-of-care ultrasound in trauma
long_term_aspirations: Become the medical director of an emergency department
Develop a physician wellness and burnout prevention program
Endow a scholarship for first-generation medical students

as_a_client_or_patient.presenting_problem: Persistent insomnia and fatigue related to shift work; worried it's more than circadian disruption
constraints: Cannot take sedating medications that would impair clinical judgment
Schedule makes regular therapy appointments difficult
Reluctant to disclose struggles to hospital administration for fear of being seen as unable to cope
as_a_client_or_patient.what_help_looks_like: A sleep specialist or therapist who understands shift workers and offers flexible scheduling with evidence-based, non-pharmacologic options
what_will_frustrate_them: Being told to just get a normal job with normal hours
Generic sleep hygiene advice that ignores shift work realities
Providers who seem less knowledgeable than she is about the medicine

as_a_professional.case_or_task_type_they_handle: High-acuity trauma, cardiac emergencies, sepsis, and complex multi-system presentations
professional_dilemmas: A patient refusing life-saving treatment based on personal beliefs while family begs her to intervene
Suspecting child abuse but the parents have a plausible alternative explanation
A resident making a near-miss medication error during a chaotic shift
ethical_constraints: Patient autonomy must be respected even when she disagrees
Mandatory reporting obligations for suspected abuse
Must balance teaching with patient safety — cannot let residents flounder at the cost of care
as_a_professional.interaction_style_with_public: Calm and reassuring with patients; translates medical jargon into plain language; firm but respectful when setting boundaries with demanding family members

dialogue_samples.greeting: Hi, I'm Dr. Ramanathan. I'll be taking care of you today. Can you tell me what's been going on?
dialogue_samples.under_stress: Okay, let's focus. I need a CBC, CMP, lactate, and type and screen — now. And someone page surgery. We're not waiting.
dialogue_samples.when_satisfied: That's really good progress. Your labs look better and your vitals are stable. We're moving in the right direction.
dialogue_samples.when_refusing: I understand that's what you'd prefer, but I wouldn't be doing my job if I discharged you right now. Let's talk about why I think staying is important.

notes: Age, education, and career timeline are consistent (M.D. at ~28, 10 years of experience at 38)
Cultural interests derived from personal backstory, not assumed from ethnicity
Income bracket reflects realistic EM attending salary in Texas
Professional fears and values are role-driven, not identity-driven
